_doc: Generated by tools/meta_analysis.py from enrichment/observations.json + the sensitivity tags in corpus_findings.json. Do not hand-edit.

weighting._doc: weight = class_base x ground_truth_mult x size_factor. Higher evidence class and more direct ground truth (SEEG / postoperative seizure-freedom) earn more weight. Transparent and tunable: change these and re-run tools/meta_analysis.py.
weighting.size_factor: 1 + log10(N)/2, capped at 2.0, when N is reported; 1.0 when N is not stated (never fabricated).

contributions.note: Ictal dysphasia/aphasia lateralized to the dominant hemisphere in ~100%.
Ictal dysphasia is dominant in 100%.
Postictal dysphasia lateralized to the dominant temporal lobe in 92% (12/13, p<0.001).
Postictal dysphasia lateralizing to the left (dominant) hemisphere in 92% (n=26, SEEG-defined subtypes).
Dysphasia lateralized to the dominant temporal lobe in 16/17 (~94%) in the n=100 ATL series.
Postictal dysphasia/aphasia dominant-hemisphere in 80-100% across series (midpoint 90).
Postictal aphasia a very reliable dominant-hemisphere sign, ~80-90% (midpoint 85).
post-ictal aphasia is strongly associated with a left dominant temporal localization.
Preserved (identifiable) ictal speech lateralized to the non-dominant side in 83% (n=12).
Ictal speech that is preserved/identifiable is non-dominant in 83%.
Ictal normal (identifiable) speech occurred in non-dominant-onset seizures (9 seizures/2 patients NHS vs 0 DHS, p=0.001).
Non-dominant in ~100% (one exception, Janszky).
Automatisms with preserved responsiveness non-dominant (right) in 62% (single-lobe-resection cohort) - lower than the review figure.
automatisms with preserved responsiveness also favor right temporal localization.
Non-dominant (right) in 81% (14/16 right-lateralized).
Ictal vomiting seen in right (non-dominant) TLE.
Epigastric sensation with ictal vomiting strongly associated with non-dominant (right) temporal onset.
ictal vomiting has been associated with right-sided temporal foci.

signs.contested: Genuinely contested: this atlas's curated card cites Kellinghaus 2003 for a DOMINANT (left) predominance, whereas the pooled sources here (Loddenkemper 2005 75% right; Fakhoury 1994 exclusively right TLE) lateralize NON-dominant. Surfaced as conflicting evidence, not reconciled.

contributions.note: Ictal spitting non-dominant (right) in 9/12 (75%).
Ictal spitting occurred exclusively in right (non-dominant) TLE - 11 (21%) of right-TLE seizures vs 0 in left TLE.
spitting and drinking automatisms suggest right temporal localization.
Wiping hand ipsilateral to the EZ in 92%.
Postictal nose-wiping ipsilateral in 62% (single-lobe-resection cohort) - lower than the review figure.
Nose-wiping performed with the hand ipsilateral to the seizure focus.
post-ictal nose wiping tends to be performed with the hand ipsilateral to the seizure focus.
Unilateral eye-blinking ipsilateral to ictal EEG onset in 85.7% (12/14).
Unilateral ictal eye-blinking ipsilateral to the EZ in 83%.
unilateral eye blinking tends to be ipsilateral to seizure origin.
Unilateral limb automatism ipsilateral to ictal EEG onset in 88.5% (23/26).
Unilateral automatisms tend to be ipsilateral to the EZ; strongest paired with contralateral dystonic posturing (the classic dyad).
Ipsilateral-automatism-plus-contralateral-posturing pattern differentiates R from L TLE.
manipulative automatisms will predominate in the extremity ipsilateral to the seizure focus.
Forced version contralateral to the EZ in 100% (Table 1; cites Wyllie/Ebner).
Contralateral versive in 61 seizures / 27 patients; ipsilateral versive never observed. Non-versive turning was non-localizing.
Version always contralateral to ictal onset (9/9, 8/8, 13/13 across groups).
Head version occurred only in the contralateral side of the EEG focus (14/14) - the ledger's earlier '89%' was a misreading of '89 seizures' (34+17+24+14, all contralateral).
Tonic head deviation contralateral to seizure focus in ~90% (n=21).
Versive (forced) deviation is contralateral; non-versive head rotation tends to be ipsilateral.
versive head turning opposite the side of seizure onset.
Unilateral tonic posturing occurred only in the contralateral side of the EEG focus (17/17).
Asymmetric (extended) side contralateral in 89%.
Unilateral tonic posturing is a 'reliable' sign (PPV > 80%); two reliable concordant signs approach 100% accuracy.
focal tonic posturing ... most often contralateral to seizure origin.
Extended elbow contralateral to ictal onset in 35/39 patients (~90%); by group 83.3% (Group I TLE), 87.5% (Group I extratemporal), 94.4% (Group II).
Extended arm contralateral in 89%.
Figure-of-4 PPV < 80% in this systematic video analysis - less reliable in isolation than version, unilateral tonic posturing, M2e, or unilateral clonic activity.
arm extension contralateral and arm flexion ipsilateral to the seizure focus (figure-of-four).
Clonic jerking occurred only in the contralateral side of the EEG focus (24/24).
Unilateral clonic activity contralateral in 83%.
Unilateral clonic activity is a 'reliable' motor sign (PPV > 80%) for contralateral lateralization.
focal clonic activity ... most often contralateral to seizure origin.
Postictal (Todd's) paresis contralateral to the EZ in 93% of 44 patients.
Todd's paresis contralateral in 93% (Kellinghaus series).
Todd's paralysis almost 100% contralateral; a reliable localizing/lateralizing motor sign.
Postictal limb paresis (Todd's) contralateral; reported in 0.5-13% of focal seizures.
Unilateral facial asymmetry in 36/50 (72%), contralateral to the focus in 31/36 (86%); and contralateral in 27/37 (73%) with a unilateral temporal focus.
Asymmetric lower facial weakness (emotional/mimetic) contralateral to the seizure focus in ~3/4 of 50 patients.
Lower facial asymmetry in ~70% of TLE, usually contralateral to the EZ.
Ictal recurrent speech with altered prosody corresponded to ictal discharge of the non-dominant precentral operculum.
Unilateral sensory aura contralateral in 89%.
Unilateral somatosensory aura contralateral in 89% of seizures.
Painful somatosensory seizures typically contralateral, localizing to the parietal operculum / insula.
sensory march is strongly suggestive of a post-central, primary sensory cortex involvement, contralateral to the ictal discharge.
Contralateral hemifield in 100% (12/42 patients with lateralized visual aura).
Ictal homonymous hemianopia / lateralized visual aura contralateral with 100% predictive value (Salanova series).
Contralateral in ~100% (single exception reported by Yen).
Dystonia occurred only in the contralateral side of the EEG focus (34/34).
Limb dystonia contralateral to the EZ in >90%.
dystonic posturing has been associated with ictal activation in the contralateral putamen.
Unilateral tonic posturing occurred only in the contralateral side of the EEG focus (17/17).
Asymmetric (extended) side contralateral in 89%.
Unilateral tonic posturing is a 'reliable' sign (PPV > 80%); two reliable concordant signs approach 100% accuracy.
focal tonic posturing ... most often contralateral to seizure origin.
Non-dominant in ~100% (one exception, Janszky).
Automatisms with preserved responsiveness non-dominant (right) in 62% (single-lobe-resection cohort) - lower than the review figure.
automatisms with preserved responsiveness also favor right temporal localization.
Extended elbow contralateral to ictal onset in 35/39 patients (~90%); by group 83.3% (Group I TLE), 87.5% (Group I extratemporal), 94.4% (Group II).
Extended arm contralateral in 89%.
Figure-of-4 PPV < 80% in this systematic video analysis - less reliable in isolation than version, unilateral tonic posturing, M2e, or unilateral clonic activity.
arm extension contralateral and arm flexion ipsilateral to the seizure focus (figure-of-four).
Forced version contralateral to the EZ in 100% (Table 1; cites Wyllie/Ebner).
Contralateral versive in 61 seizures / 27 patients; ipsilateral versive never observed. Non-versive turning was non-localizing.
Version always contralateral to ictal onset (9/9, 8/8, 13/13 across groups).
Head version occurred only in the contralateral side of the EEG focus (14/14) - the ledger's earlier '89%' was a misreading of '89 seizures' (34+17+24+14, all contralateral).
Tonic head deviation contralateral to seizure focus in ~90% (n=21).
Versive (forced) deviation is contralateral; non-versive head rotation tends to be ipsilateral.
versive head turning opposite the side of seizure onset.
Contralateral hemifield in 100% (12/42 patients with lateralized visual aura).
Ictal homonymous hemianopia / lateralized visual aura contralateral with 100% predictive value (Salanova series).
Ictal dysphasia/aphasia lateralized to the dominant hemisphere in ~100%.
Ictal dysphasia is dominant in 100%.
Ictal recurrent speech with altered prosody corresponded to ictal discharge of the non-dominant precentral operculum.

sensitivity.method: Sensitivity = P(sign | localization): the frequency of the sign within a single localization group. Figures are the verified frequency-within-a-group findings in the source ledger, tagged to their sign. Population-specific, so reported per (sign, localization) rather than pooled across groups; mean shown when a group has >1 source, otherwise the single value with its k.
sensitivity.note_specificity: Specificity requires the sign's rate in the OTHER localization groups (the false-positive side), which this corpus reports for essentially no sign (1 figure(s) corpus-wide). Card specificity is therefore a curator teaching estimate, marked 'est.', not computed here.

sources.cite: Loddenkemper & Kotagal 2005
sources.quote: Clonic activity [53] | 44.4% FLE | 83% contralateral | Brodmann areas 4 and 6

sources.cite: Loddenkemper & Kotagal 2005
sources.quote: Janszky et al. found unilateral clonic activity in 33% of patients with frontal lobe epilepsy rendered seizure free after epilepsy surgery. Clonic activity was ipsilateral to the seizure focus in 2 and contralateral in 10 patients [53].

sources.cite: Loddenkemper & Kotagal 2005
sources.quote: Ebner et al., who evaluated 123 patients with temporal lobe epilepsy ... 7 of whom had automatisms and preserved consciousness (5.7%). Automatisms with preserved responsiveness were observed exclusively during seizures arising from the right nondominant temporal lobe and occurred in 10% of these patients.

sources.cite: Loddenkemper & Kotagal 2005
sources.quote: Hemifield visual aura [26] | 28.6% OLE | 100% contralateral | Brodmann areas 17-19 and adjacent areas

sources.cite: Loddenkemper & Kotagal 2005
sources.quote: Version [46,53] | 22.2% FLE | 100% contralateral | Brodmann areas 6 and 8

sources.cite: Loddenkemper & Kotagal 2005
sources.quote: Tonic activity [53] | 48.1% FLE | 89% contralateral | SMA, possibly also Brodmann area 6, the anterior cingulate gyrus, and subcortical structures

sources.cite: Loddenkemper & Kotagal 2005
